I Watched a Senior Engineer Fail a System Design Interview. He Was Better Than the Engineer Who Got the Offer.

The interview wasn’t testing what he thought it was testing. Neither was the one I gave him two years earlier when I hired him.

10 min read
Just now

--

I have been on both sides of the system design interview enough times to know that the outcome is not primarily determined by technical knowledge.
Daniel knew this intellectually. He had read the right books. He had done the mock interviews. He had studied distributed systems concepts with the kind of thoroughness that produces engineers who can explain the nuances of consistent hashing to someone who has never heard of it.
He walked into that interview more prepared than anyone I have managed before or since.
He did not get the offer.
The engineer who did get the offer, I found out later through a mutual contact, had three fewer years of experience. Had not read the same books. Could not have explained consistent hashing with the same depth.
But he had something Daniel didn’t. Or rather, he did something Daniel didn’t. In the interview room, under pressure, with the clock running and someone watching, he did the thing that system design interviews are actually designed to surface.
Daniel designed a system. The other engineer made his thinking visible while designing a system.
Those are different activities. They look similar from the outside. They produce completely different signals to the person sitting across the table.

What I Watched Happen in the Interview
I was not in Daniel’s interview. But I debriefed him afterward, for almost two hours, because I wanted to understand what happened and because he deserved more than a rejection email.
He walked me through the problem. A URL shortener with specific scale requirements. He walked me through his solution. It was technically excellent. The data model was correct. The scaling approach was sound. The tradeoffs he had made were defensible and in most cases optimal.
I asked him what the interviewer had said during the session.
Not much, he told me. Asked a few clarifying questions early on. Mostly listened while Daniel designed.
I asked him what he had said during the session.
And here is where the picture became clear.
He had explained his design. He had described each component. He had answered the questions asked. He had done everything correctly in the technical sense.
He had not narrated his reasoning. He had not named the tradeoffs as he made them. He had not said out loud what he was optimizing for and what he was sacrificing. He had not surfaced the constraints he was establishing before he started drawing boxes. He had not explained why he was making each choice instead of the alternatives he was considering and rejecting.
He had given the interviewer a correct answer to a question the interviewer was not asking.
The interviewer was not asking: can you design a URL shortener?
The interviewer was asking: can I see how you think when you design a system?
Those are different questions. The second one requires the same technical knowledge as the first one plus the ability to externalize the reasoning in real time. Daniel had the technical knowledge. He had not developed the habit of externalizing it.

The Two Years Before the Interview
I hired Daniel two years before that interview happened.
I hired him because his technical depth was the strongest I had seen in that hiring round. His system design in our interview was correct. His answers to technical questions were precise. His debugging instincts during the coding portion were sharp.
I did not ask myself, during that hiring process, whether Daniel’s reasoning was visible or whether I was inferring it from correct outputs.
I was inferring it. The outputs were correct so I assumed the reasoning was sound. I was right about the reasoning. I was not thinking about whether Daniel had developed the habit of making it explicit.
For two years he worked on my team. He was one of the strongest engineers I had. His systems were well-designed. His incident response was fast. His architecture decisions were defensible and usually correct.
In one-on-ones I could see his thinking. He articulated it clearly when I asked the right questions. The thinking was always there.
I never noticed that the thinking was only visible when I asked for it. That left to his own defaults, in a meeting or a design review or an interview, Daniel presented conclusions rather than reasoning. Answers rather than process. Systems rather than the thinking behind them.
That gap between conclusions and reasoning does not matter in most daily engineering work. The work speaks for itself when there is time for it to speak.
In an interview, there is no time. The interviewer sees forty-five minutes of behavior and makes a judgment from that. If the behavior in those forty-five minutes is presenting conclusions without visible reasoning, the judgment is that the reasoning may not be there, regardless of whether the conclusions are correct.

What the Interviewer Is Actually Watching
I have sat on the interviewer side of enough system design loops to describe exactly what I am watching and what changes my assessment.
I am not watching whether the candidate arrives at a correct design. I already know the correct design. I wrote the problem.
I am watching how the candidate handles the moments where the problem is underspecified. Every system design problem is underspecified on purpose. The constraints that determine the design are not given. They have to be established. The candidate who establishes them explicitly, by asking specific questions about scale and consistency requirements and access patterns before drawing anything, is demonstrating that they know what determines a design. The candidate who starts drawing immediately is demonstrating that they don’t know what they don’t know.
I am watching how the candidate names tradeoffs as they make them. Not whether they make the right tradeoffs. Whether they name them. “I’m choosing a relational database here because our access patterns are predictable and we need transactional guarantees. The tradeoff is that we’re accepting a higher operational complexity ceiling if we need to shard later.” That sentence tells me something about how this person thinks in architecture meetings, in design reviews, in the conversations where the actual engineering decisions get made. A candidate who makes the same choice without naming the reasoning tells me nothing.
I am watching how the candidate handles the moment I introduce a new constraint midway through. Every system design interview has this moment. The scale is ten times larger than assumed. A new requirement appears. A constraint changes. The candidate who treats this as a problem has revealed that their design was a fixed answer rather than a reasoning process. The candidate who treats it as new information and updates the design while explaining what changes and what doesn’t has revealed something much more valuable.
I am watching what the candidate does when they don’t know something. The confident wrong answer tells me the candidate cannot calibrate their own uncertainty. The honest acknowledgment of uncertainty followed by a structured approach to reasoning toward an answer tells me the candidate can operate in the conditions that actual senior engineering requires.
Daniel’s design was correct. His tradeoffs were sound. His constraint handling was good. None of that was visible because he presented the design rather than the designing.

The Habit That Changes the Interview
The candidate who does well in system design interviews consistently is not the candidate who knows the most. It is the candidate who has developed a specific habit that most engineers never develop because it is never required in daily work.
The habit is narrating the reasoning in real time, out loud, including the uncertainty, including the alternatives considered and rejected, including the explicit acknowledgment of what is being optimized for and what is being sacrificed.
In daily work, this habit is optional. The design document captures the reasoning. The code review surfaces the tradeoffs. The architecture review gives time for questions. The reasoning can be reconstructed from the outputs if necessary.
In an interview, the habit is the only way the reasoning becomes visible. The interviewer cannot reconstruct it from the outputs. They see forty-five minutes of behavior. If the behavior does not include explicit reasoning, the reasoning is invisible regardless of its quality.
Building the habit requires practicing something that feels unnatural and slightly uncomfortable. Most engineers are trained to think before they speak. The interview habit requires thinking while speaking. Letting the reasoning be visible while it is incomplete. Naming uncertainties before they are resolved. Stating hypotheses before they are confirmed.
The discomfort is real. It feels like showing your work before you know if the work is right. It feels vulnerable in a specific way that engineering culture does not typically reward.
It is also the only thing that separates the candidate who demonstrates senior judgment from the candidate who has senior judgment but demonstrates a correct answer.

What I Should Have Done Differently
I spent a long time after Daniel’s interview thinking about my role in it.
Not in the interview itself. I was not there. In the two years before it.
I had watched Daniel work. I had seen his reasoning. I had never named the habit that was missing because I had never noticed it was missing. His outputs were correct. His reasoning was sound when I asked for it. I had not noticed that asking for it was doing work that should have been automatic.
The feedback I could have given Daniel, consistently, over two years, was specific and would have cost almost nothing.
“In that design review, you presented the final architecture. Next time, walk the room through why you eliminated the alternatives. Not the conclusion. The reasoning.”
“In that incident post-mortem, you identified the root cause quickly. In the write-up, include the hypotheses you generated and eliminated. Not just what happened. How you thought about what might be happening.”
“In that architecture meeting, you made the right call. Before the next one, spend five minutes thinking about how you would explain the tradeoff to someone who wasn’t in the room. Not the answer. The reasoning behind it.”
These are not complicated instructions. They are the habit, named explicitly, repeated consistently, until the habit is automatic rather than something that requires prompting.
I did not give that feedback because I was not looking for that gap. I was looking at outputs. The outputs were correct. I stopped there.
Daniel paid for that gap in a job offer he should have had.

The Engineer Who Got the Offer
The engineer who got the offer over Daniel — I thought about him for a while after I heard about it.
He was less technically deep. Daniel was better in almost every technical dimension I can measure.
But he had developed, somehow, the habit of making his reasoning visible. Whether through practice, through coaching, through a manager who had told him explicitly what the habit looked like, I don’t know.
What I know is that in forty-five minutes of a system design interview, his reasoning was visible and Daniel’s was not. And that visibility produced the offer that Daniel’s technical depth did not.
The outcome was not fair in the sense that the more technically capable engineer did not get the job. It was accurate in the sense that the interview was measuring something specific and the engineer who demonstrated it got the result.
The thing it was measuring — the ability to make reasoning visible under pressure, in real time, while the stakes are real — is not a performance skill sepa
